Summary of what was found: PPC’s grade overview gives a useful baseline for beauty cartons: SBS is premium white board for cosmetics and high-end retail packs; CUK/kraft is strength-led; CRB is recycled and value-oriented; chipboard is used for rigid/setup luxury goods boxes including cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: SBS, CUK, CRB, and chipboard/setup box board. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and rigid boxes. Weight/material reduction evidence: no quantified reduction, but it identifies rigid/setup boxes made from thick chipboard as a heavier format risk area and frames folding-carton grades as alternatives. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: SBS is identified for cosmetics; rigid boxes are identified for small luxury goods including cosmetics. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe aligns with SBS and rigid chipboard boxes; mainstream aligns with CRB and CUK depending on strength and appearance. Limitation/caution: classification source rather than quantified downgauging case; use as baseline taxonomy.
Source name: 4 Types of Paperboard — Paperboard Packaging Council — https://paperbox.org/4-types-of-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging separates Consumer Beauty from Luxury Beauty: mass-market beauty focuses on shelf impact, while luxury beauty emphasizes premium finishes, structural design, anti-counterfeit embellishments, gift boxes, inserts, windows, and special effects.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: beauty folding cartons and gift-box systems using paperboard plus finishing layers. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: standard folding cartons, gift boxes, inserts, windows, metallic/tactile finishes. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a grammage source, but explains why premium cartons often accumulate extra material through structure and finishes, while consumer beauty uses scalable carton formats. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, fragrances, skincare, cosmetics, and hair color are directly covered. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong evidence for splitting luxury and consumer tracks. Limitation/caution: solutions page, not technical board-spec page; pair with grade and recyclability sources.

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging confirms SBS/bleached paperboard as a bright-white, high-print-performance board for high-end beauty and personal care cartons, with strong converting performance for die-cutting, foil stamping, embossing, folding, and gluing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: solid bleached sulfate/bleached paperboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: high-end folding cartons and branded outer packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a downgauging case; establishes why SBS remains common in premium beauty due to white shade, graphics, advanced finishing, and converting performance. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty and personal care are explicit applications. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe incumbent board baseline against which lighter FBB alternatives should be tested. Limitation/caution: supports appearance/finish need, not automatic lightweighting suitability.

Summary of what was found: Holmen’s Invercote G shows how premium SBB/SBS boards are engineered for prestige cartons: ultra-smooth, triple-coated, high whiteness, and suitable for foil, film lamination, embossing, and aroma-sensitive products.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: premium SBB/SBS cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: prestige folding cartons for luxury cosmetics, fragrance, and high-end packs. Weight/material reduction evidence: used as heavier-feature baseline rather than lightweight route; shows why premium beauty often remains with coated SBB/SBS because the substrate supports sophisticated imagery, foil/film lamination, and sensory quality. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: prestige packaging and aroma-sensitive products are explicit. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: film lamination and luxury finishes can raise material intensity and recyclability complexity if overused.
Source name: Invercote G Technical Data Sheet — Holmen Board & Paper — https://www.holmen.com/en/board-and-paper/products/paperboard/invercote/invercote-g/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s luxury-carton page reinforces that premium beauty and perfume cartons often prioritize tactile feel and demanding finishes, including velvet-like uncoated SBS, coated SBS, embossing, foil stamping, metallized foils, and high-end converting.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: luxury SBS boards, coated and uncoated. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: premium folding cartons for beauty and fragrances. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a downgauging source; explains why lightweighting luxury cartons can be difficult because premium value is often built through white boards, coatings, velvet-touch surfaces, embossing, foils, and two-sided coatings. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, personal care, and perfumes are explicit. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: use as filter for routes that may compromise premium haptics or decoration quality if downgauged aggressively.
Source name: Coated and uncoated carton boards for luxury packaging — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/carton-boards-for-luxury-packaging

Summary of what was found: Metsä Board explains FBB lightweighting through a three-layer structure: chemical pulp outer layers plus a BCTMP/high-yield middle layer that delivers high caliper, bulk, and stiffness at lower weight; beautycare is listed as a core end use.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight fresh-fibre folding boxboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: cosmetic folding cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: source states lightweight folding boxboards consume less raw material, reduce transport weight, and produce less waste; lower weight is tied to three-layer structure and high-yield pulp middle layer. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beautycare is named. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for both, especially where brands want premium printability without defaulting to heavier SBS. Limitation/caution: barrier-coated grades should only be used where barrier performance is actually required.

Summary of what was found: Holmen’s Incada Exel describes a fresh-fibre FBB with lightweight construction, high stiffness, low density, high-quality printing, varnishing, and high-speed conversion, making it a candidate premium-but-lighter alternative to heavier bleached boards.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: lightweight FBB. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: quality folding cartons for cosmetics and high-end applications. Weight/material reduction evidence: low density plus high stiffness; described as lightweight and optimized for visual impact and conversion. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: Holmen’s Incada family is positioned for cosmetics, and Exel is its premium FBB member. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong where premium appearance is needed but full SBS/SBB may be over-specified. Limitation/caution: still a premium-grade board; check whiteness, finishing depth, and fragrance-carton expectations before substitution.
Source name: Incada Exel Technical Data Sheet — Holmen Board & Paper — https://www.holmen.com/en/board-and-paper/products/paperboard/incada/incada-exel/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s Performa family is positioned for premium folding cartons in beauty and personal care, with lightweight GC1/GC2 options; Performa Light is its lightest-weight GC2 grade, while Performa White and Nova offer high stiffness and reduced weight for fragrances, cosmetics, and personal care.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: high-bulk/high-stiffness lightweight FBB for premium cartons. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: premium beauty, fragrance, and personal-care folding cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: multilevel board structures provide higher strength and stiffness at lower weight; Performa Nova is described as engineered to carry more with less. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty, personal care, fragrances, and cosmetics are named uses. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium route for preserving print and surface quality while reducing reliance on heavier SBS specifications. Limitation/caution: selection must be linked to actual decoration needs; heavy lamination or deep foil/embossing may limit one-for-one interchangeability.
Source name: Premium folding carton boards for packaging / Performa by Stora Enso — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons ; https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/paperboard-materials/folding-cartons/performa

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s Tambrite is described as a packaging workhorse for everyday necessities, with high stiffness, material efficiency, and an ideal stiffness-to-weight ratio; a customer example reports a 33% basis-weight reduction when switching from recycled board to Tambrite.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: GC2 folding boxboard optimized for efficiency and runnability. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: scalable cartons where cost, line performance, and adequate appearance matter more than prestige extremes. Weight/material reduction evidence: 33% lower basis weight versus a recycled-board baseline while improving visual characteristics and packing-line efficiency. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: direct beauty references are limited, but it is a strong analog for personal-care and mass-market secondary cartons. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market/consumer beauty and personal care. Limitation/caution: transfer to cosmetics should still check whiteness, tactile expectations, and premium visual standards.

Summary of what was found: Graphic Packaging’s recycled paperboard page positions CRB as a lightweight sheet for most consumer-product folding cartons, with 100% recycled fiber, double-clay-coated print surfaces, and grades whose brightness/whiteness can compete with bleached paperboard.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: CRB/WLC recycled paperboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: mass-market folding cartons and substitution where premium boards are over-specified. Weight/material reduction evidence: PaceSetter and Conquest CRB grades are described as lightweight sheets with print/performance benefits. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: wide consumer folding-carton use is relevant to personal care and accessible beauty. Premium versus mass-market fit: best for mass-market; selected grades may be useful where recycled content is desired without losing too much brightness/whiteness. Limitation/caution: check fragrance-carton purity, premium whiteness, embossing, and foil requirements before substituting premium board.

Summary of what was found: MM’s WLC page confirms that coated recycled board/white-lined chipboard is a mainstream recycled-cartonboard option and that MM markets it into cosmetics and personal care, not only household categories.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: WLC/CRB recycled cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons for cosmetics and personal care plus other consumer categories. Weight/material reduction evidence: source demonstrates availability and suitability rather than quantified downgauging. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: cosmetics and personal care are explicitly named. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and selected mid-tier products; useful in cost-led and recycled-content tracks. Limitation/caution: pair with appearance and printability qualification for brands needing high whiteness or luxury finishes.
Source name: Recycled Cartonboard (WLC Board) — MM Board & Paper — https://mm.group/board-paper/products/cartonboard-wlc/

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s CKB page shows coated unbleached kraft as a strong, lightweight, material-efficient route and documents a customer case reaching lower basis weight and 15% packaging-weight savings; CKB Nude adds a natural brown premium look without conventional plastic barriers.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: CUK/CNK-style kraft-back cartonboard. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: strength-led folding cartons and natural-look premium cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: virgin kraft-fiber strength is linked to material efficiency and low weight; customer case quotes 15% packaging-weight saving from lower basis-weight solution. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: not cosmetic-specific, but natural brown reverse and premium natural-look positioning fit clean beauty, apothecary, and eco-premium brands. Premium versus mass-market fit: both; mass-market where strength is needed at lower basis weight and premium where natural kraft aesthetics are acceptable. Limitation/caution: unsuitable where ultra-white luxury surface is required inside and out.

Summary of what was found: Walki’s barrier-board page shows a route for reducing plastic lamination complexity in cartons: fibre-based boards with tailored coatings that preserve printability/converting and can reduce or eliminate inner bags or liners while maintaining paper-stream recyclability in many cases.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: barrier-coated fibre-based folding-carton boards. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons needing moisture, grease, oxygen, aroma, or light protection, and beauty cartons needing limited resistance to staining or migration from oily products. Weight/material reduction evidence: structures are designed to reduce or eliminate additional inner bags or plastic liners and simplify packaging structures while maintaining performance. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: mostly food/household oriented, but material logic transfers to secondary beauty packs relying on laminated or complex substrates. Premium versus mass-market fit: mass-market and premium where barrier performance is needed but full plastic lamination is undesirable. Limitation/caution: film, foil, and metallized laminations should not automatically be treated as preferred lightweight/recyclable routes.
Source name: Barrier Boards for Folding Cartons | Recyclable Fibre-Based Packaging | WalkiPack — Walki — https://www.walki.com/products/walki-pack-barrier-boards-folding-cartons/

Summary of what was found: AF&PA’s recyclability guidance shows that water-soluble inks/adhesives, clay coatings, and varnish coatings are generally easier for mills, while hot-melt adhesives, laminated foils, stamped foils, metallized foils, and wet-strength resins are more likely to create recycling challenges.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: inks, adhesives, coatings, foils, windows, and non-fiber elements on paperboard cartons. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: bleached paperboard cartons, recycled/unbleached boxboard cartons, and carrier stock cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: not a basis-weight source; critical for screening because lighter cartons can become less circular if they rely on problematic non-fibre elements. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to beauty cartons using foils, windows, and decorative effects. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium cartons are the main exposure area due to specialty finishes; mass-market can usually stay closer to easier-to-recycle coating and adhesive systems. Limitation/caution: recycling challenge does not automatically mean non-recyclable; mill capability varies.
Source name: Design Guidance for Recyclability for Paper-based Packaging — American Forest & Paper Association — https://www.afandpa.org/sites/default/files/2021-08/AFPADesignGuidanceforRecyclability_FINAL_031621.pdf

Summary of what was found: 4evergreen’s guideline gives carton-design guidance: avoid full-surface metallization; PP/PET metallized laminates and PET metallized films can disrupt NIR sorting; strongly attached windows should be avoided; detachable, thin, lightweight windows are preferred. It links design to PPWR weight-minimization and recyclability expectations.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: decorative metallization, metallized laminates, films, and windows on fibre-based packaging. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and fibre-based composite packaging. Weight/material reduction evidence: most useful as a filter for decorative/material choices that add non-fibre layers and impair sorting even if fibre content remains dominant; recommends thin lightweight detachable windows where needed. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: highly relevant to fragrance and premium skincare cartons using metallic effects and windows. Premium versus mass-market fit: main risk area is premium/luxe. Limitation/caution: recommendations are based on expert opinion and recyclability behavior depends on chemistry/formulation, so case-specific testing remains necessary.
Source name: Circularity by Design Guideline for Paper-based Packaging — 4evergreen — https://4evergreenforum.eu/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/4evergreen-Circularity-by-Design-Version-4-February-2026.pdf

Summary of what was found: Stora Enso’s packaging-performance page makes a key screening point: carton lightweighting is not simply lower gsm; it means achieving required stiffness and structural performance at lower grammage, and final packaging performance must be validated through methods such as box-compression testing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: stiffness-to-weight optimization and packaging performance testing logic. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons and boxes across the value chain. Weight/material reduction evidence: lightweighting is defined as lower grammage with maintained stiffness/structural performance; thickness, bulk, and grammage alone do not show finished package behavior. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: directly transferable to cosmetic cartons where downgauging risks crushed corners, poor shelf appearance, or weak stacking. Premium versus mass-market fit: both. Limitation/caution: methodology/explanatory source rather than board-spec source. Use in screening: frame how later carton candidates are evaluated.
Source name: Better Board, Better Performance — Stora Enso — https://www.storaenso.com/en/products/packaging-offering/better-board-better-performance

Summary of what was found: PPC’s paperboard-properties article identifies the board attributes needed to qualify lighter cartons: basis weight, caliper, glueability, plybond strength, smoothness, and Taber stiffness in machine and cross directions.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: board-property and qualification metrics. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons. Weight/material reduction evidence: no downgauging example, but useful for evidence-matrix design because it identifies the variables to track when switching to a lighter grade. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: beauty cartons can fail on print smoothness, stiffness, glueability, or curl rather than nominal gsm; this source supports that screening logic. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium packs care more about brightness and smoothness; mass-market packs emphasize stiffness, glueability, runnability, and cost. Limitation/caution: educational industry article; use for evaluation criteria rather than proof any board grade passes.
Source name: The Properties of Paperboard — Paperboard Packaging Council — https://paperbox.org/the-properties-of-paperboard/

Summary of what was found: TAPPI’s score-bend method shows why folding performance must be checked when cartons are downgauged: the test helps carton makers, converters, printers, and filling operations evaluate how a carton performs on package filling and sealing lines.
Detailed description of what was found: Material or technology route covered: score-bend and folding-line qualification. Carton/secondary-pack format relevance: folding cartons running on filling and sealing lines. Weight/material reduction evidence: no direct basis-weight data, but highly relevant because thinner or lower-density boards can fail at creases and folds even when print or stiffness looks acceptable. Cosmetic/personal care relevance: important for high-speed mass-market cartoning and premium cartons with complex folds or embellished scores. Premium versus mass-market fit: both; especially mass-market line efficiency and premium crack-prone embellished cartons. Limitation/caution: standards-method source defining testing approach rather than pass/fail values for specific cosmetic boards.
Source name: Score bend test, Test Method TAPPI/ANSI T 577 om-21 — TAPPI — https://imisrise.tappi.org/TAPPI/Products/01/T/0104T577.aspx
